adm1_overview: It's capital is Kisumu, the third-largest city in Kenya; major commercial and transport hub on Lake Victoria. Economy driven by sugarcane, fishing, rice irrigation, and trade. Kisumu County is relatively densely populated compared with the rest of Kenya. In 2023, it was an epicenter of political violence: security forces used teargas and live ammunition against opposition-led demonstrations, with ACLED recording it as a primary hotspot alongside Nairobi.

Central highland county in the Mt. Kenya region; economy anchored in coffee, tea, horticulture, and dairy. Historically the heartland of the Mau Mau independence movement against British colonial rule (1950s). The Kieni sub-counties face periodic food insecurity (IPC Phase 2), driven by frost, failed rains, and livestock pests. Generally stable and low-conflict rate.

One of Kenya's smallest and most densely populated counties in the western region; agriculture (tea, maize, bananas) dominates. The county is part of the Lake Region Economic Bloc (LREB) established in 2018 to foster regional economic, industrial, social, and technological collaboration. High out-migration due to land pressure and limited economic opportunity. Low direct conflict footprint, though affected by election-related tensions.

Densely populated highland county; tea, banana, and soapstone carving are economic mainstays. ACLED identifies the Nyanza region (including Kisii) as accounting for over 40% of police intervention in Azimio-led demonstrations in 2023. Inter-county border dispute with Nyamira has turned violent in 2023. Experienced significant post-election violence during the 2007-2008 crisis.

Rural county on the eastern Mt. Kenya slopes; miraa (khat), mango, and subsistence farming are economic pillars. Relatively low conflict intensity. The Maarifa Centre (Council of Governors) documents ongoing development challenges around water access and agricultural productivity in its County Integrated Development Plans.

Lakeside county on Lake Victoria's shores; fishing and subsistence agriculture are primary livelihoods. The county is part of the Lake Region Economic Bloc (LREB). ACLED records Siaya as one of six Nyanza counties where Azimio-led demonstrations faced heavy police intervention in 2023. Affected by 2007-2008 post-election violence.

National capital and Kenya's largest city; political and economic center of the country. ACLED identifies Nairobi as the county with the most political violence events nationwide, recording the highest number of mob violence incidents and demonstration events in 2022 and 2023. Significant informal settlement population (e.g., Kibera). High inequality and recurring unrest tied to election cycles and anti-government protests.

Small highland county in Nyanza; tea and banana farming are the dominant economic activities. The county is part of the Lake Region Economic Bloc (LREB). ACLED groups Nyamira within the Nyanza bloc that accounted for over 40% of police intervention in 2023 Azimio demonstrations. Inter-county border dispute with Kisii has produced violent incidents in 2023.

Kenya's second city; economy driven by trade, tourism, and logistics. In addition to the coffee trade, the food and chemical industries, Mombasa has a steel mill, an aluminum rolling mill, an oil refinery and a cement plant. The city is home to the most important seaport in East Africa, Kilindini Harbour. At 44%, the rate of youth unemployment in Mombasa is more than double the national average of 21% (2016). ACLED ranks Mombasa among Kenya's key urban protest centers, with demonstrations in 2023-2024 linked to anti-government movements. Al-Shabaab-linked radicalization and recruitment have been documented since 2011 in coastal areas. Security assessment notes elevated insecurity in informal areas.

Kenya's most populous western county; sugarcane, maize, and Kakamega Forest (Kenya's only tropical rainforest). ACLED records Kakamega as one of the top five counties by political violence events in 2022, with mob violence events peaking during the election campaign period. Experienced 2007-2008 post-election violence and ongoing election-cycle tensions.

Eastern Mt. Kenya slopes county; coffee, tea, and macadamia are key cash crops. Embu County is one of the most economically stable counties in Kenya (fourth largest GCP per capita in Kenya). Relatively peaceful county with low direct conflict footprint. Government food security assessments identify the area as generally food secure with occasional stress during drought years.

Southeastern county bordering Tanzania; home to Tsavo East and West National Parks, driving tourism alongside sisal and horticulture. UNICEF Kenya 2022 Humanitarian Situation Report identifies Taita Taveta among counties where agricultural land was destroyed by floods.

Lakeshore county with economic activity in fishing and subsistence agriculture. It has one of Kenya's highest HIV/AIDS prevalence rates. ACLED records Homa Bay as one of the Nyanza counties where Azimio Coalition demonstrations were concentrated in May 2023. Experienced post-election violence in 2007-2008 crisis.

Border county with Tanzania and Lake Victoria; gold mining (Macalder), fishing, and sugarcane economy. Migori town serves as in important link between Kenya and Tanzania and the second most viable commercial center in Luo-Nyanza after Kisumu. ACLED records Migori as one of the six Nyanza counties with high Azimio demonstration activity in 2023. Periodic election-related violence and gold trade-linked insecurity.
Fertile central county on the southern Mt. Kenya slopes; Kenya's top rice producer alongside tea cultivation. Generally peaceful with low conflict history.
Coffee and tea farming county in central Kenya's highlands. Historically significant during the Mau Mau independence era. Relatively stable. Low direct conflict footprint in recent ACLED data.

Kenya's premier tea-producing county in the Rift Valley; economy anchored in large tea estates. ACLED recorded violent inter-ethnic clashes in October 2023 along the Kisumu-Kericho border. Scene of severe 2007-2008 post-election violence.
Peri-urban county bordering Nairobi; fast-growing economy driven by floriculture, real estate, and coffee. One of the highest GCP per capita rates. Generally stable with urban insecurity risks.
Semi-arid county with horticulture, stone carving activities. Hosts the large technology hub, Konza Technopolis development site. Generally peaceful. Low conflict footprint in recent ACLED records.

Major Rift Valley county; mixed farming (wheat, dairy, pyrethrum) and tourism (Lake Nakuru). ACLED lists Nakuru among counties affected by mob violence during the 2022 election period. Affected by 2007-2008 post-election violence including ethnic displacement.

Border county with Uganda; cross-border trade is the dominant economic driver. The county is part of the Lake Region Economic Bloc (LREB). UNICEF's 2022 Kenya humanitarian report identified Busia among counties with agricultural land destroyed by flooding. Generally low direct conflict intensity.

Western county; sugarcane, maize, and Webuye paper mill. The area experiences high rainfall throughout the year and is home to several large rivers, which are used for small-scale irrigation. ACLED records Bungoma as one of the border dispute-affected counties where inter-community tensions have been identified in 2023 and 2024. Experienced 2007-2008 post-election violence.

Arid and semi-arid county in eastern Kenya; mango, cotton, and drought-resilient crops. Relatively peaceful. ACLED notes Makueni as one location where politically-linked killings were reported during 2023 Azimio protest. It remains a low-intensity county overall.

Breadbasket county; wheat and maize heartland anchored in Eldoret city. The county capital, Eldoret, is home to a textile industry. The region has witnessed tensions during various political
periods, including the transition to multiparty politics in 1991, and subsequent elections in 1997 and 2007. A relative calm was observed during the 2013 and 2017 election periods, which is largely attributed to the political realignments that temporarily unified the main conflicting groups, the Kalenjin and Kikuyu communities.  Some land-displacement issues remain unresolved.

Fertile north-eastern Mt. Kenya county; miraa (khat) is the dominant cash crop. ACLED reported the government expanding Operation Maliza Uhalifu into Meru in late 2023 following requests from local leaders concerned about banditry spillover from Isiolo. Generally peaceful.

Rift Valley county; tea and dairy farming. The county is part of the Lake Region Economic Bloc (LREB). ACLED documented reoccuring violent inter-ethnic clashes along the Kisumu-Nandi border in 2016 onwards. Scene of 2007-2008 post-election ethnic violence.

High-altitude central county; wheat, potato, and dairy farming in the Aberdare ranges. Fast growing economy. Low current conflict footprint; food security reports note seasonal frost as a production risk.

Fertile 'food basket' county bordering Uganda; maize and wheat production. ACLED records Trans Nzoia among the border dispute-affected counties (Turkana-Trans Nzoia boundary tensions) flagged in 2023 Senate reports. Multi-ethnic; experienced 2007-2008 post-election violence.

The main economic activity carried out in the county is mixed farming, which consists mainly of livestock and subsistence farming. ACLED's Operation Maliza Uhalifu reporting (launched Feb 2023) identifies Elgeyo Marakwet as one of six target counties for the security operation against pastoralist militias.
Semi-arid Maasai land rapidly urbanizing near Nairobi; livestock herding and horticulture. Urban expansion onto communal Maasai lands is driving displacement and intensifying land tenure disputes

Tea farming county in the southwestern Rift Valley; largely agrarian economy. The county is characterized by its rolling hills, fertile agricultural land, and significant natural resources, including the Mau Forest and rivers such as Nyangores and Chepalungu.  The county is part of the Lake Region Economic Bloc (LREB). Low conflict profile in recent ACLED data.

Semi-arid plateau; private wildlife conservancies, ranching, and horticulture. ACLED's December 2023 situation update documents a Pokot-Samburu peace agreement in Laikipia North as part of 25 local peace agreements in 2023 — highlighting chronic inter-communal resource conflict. Major escalations of herder incursions on private conservancies occurred in 2017 and 2021.

Coastal archipelago county with UNESCO World Heritage status (Lamu Old Town); tourism and fishing. Migration driven by Lamu Port South Sudan-Ethiopia Transport (LAPSSET) opportunities is fueling challenging population growth in Lamu. ACLED's September 2023 update identifies Lamu as having the highest number of reported fatalities in Kenya (nearly 95) during July-September 2023, driven by intensified al-Shabaab attacks on civilians and security forces. Ongoing insurgency since ~2011; Boni Forest used as a militant hideout.

Large semi-arid eastern county; coal deposits (Mui Basin), cotton, and honey production. ACLED records Kitui-Tana River as one of the inter-county border disputes that turned violent in 2023. Chronic food insecurity and drought vulnerability; generally low conflict intensity.

Semi-arid gateway to northern Kenya; pastoralism and growing tourism. ACLED's January 2024 update notes that the Interior Ministry announced expansion of Operation Maliza Uhalifu into Isiolo following rising pastoralist militia vioelence. Inter-ethnic resource conflicts (Borana, Somali, Turkana communities) are recurring.

Coastal county; tourism, coconut, and cashew farming. ACLED security assessment identifies Kilifi as part of the coastal al-Shabaab influence zone with documented radicalization since ~2012. According to the National Police Service (NPS), Kilifi County recorded the highest number of land-related crimes nationwide between 2014 and 2021, with thirty-four reported land invasions and 71 related criminal offences.

Home to the Maasai Mara National Reserve; tourism is the economic engine alongside Maasai pastoralism. ACLED lists Narok among counties with county border disputes. Farmer-herder land conflicts and periodic election-linked violence are documented.

Rift Valley county; pastoralism and fishing (Lake Baringo). ACLED's August 2023 report on Operation Maliza Uhalifu identifies Baringo as one of six target counties for the North Rift security operation, with military airstrikes conducted against militia hideouts on Tandare hills in March 2023.

Coastal county; titanium mining, tourism, and cashew. In late 2021, the Kenyan government and Red Cross launched livestock off-take and cash transfer programs to help Kwale County residents survive severe drought. ACLED security assessment flags Kwale within the coastal al-Shabaab influence and recruitment zone.

Northeastern arid county. The economy is primarily based on agriculture, livestock farming, and trade.  ACLED records Garissa as consistently among Kenya's highest-fatality counties for al-Shabaab activity: 22 fatalities recorded Sept-Nov 2023 alone; site of the 2015 Garissa University massacre (148 killed). Al-Shabaab uses Boni Forest as a hideout for cross-border attacks.

Remote arid county on the Ethiopian border; pastoralism (camels, cattle). ACLED's June 2023 situation update records Marsabit with the one of the highest fatality count of any Kenyan county during the April-June 2023 period, attributed to ethnic militia clashes. Chronic inter-ethnic conflict over water and pasture.

Arid northeastern county. Wajir County is among the counties with the highest populations of livestock from which the community source their livelihoods. ACLED documents Wajir as a consistent al-Shabaab operating county. Porous border with Somalia enables cross-border militant movement.

Semi-arid northwestern county. The West Pokot county economy is principally driven by livestock rearing and agriculture. ACLED’s December 2023 update highlights West Pokot as a key area of concern due to ongoing pastoral militia attacks. West Pokot showed increased militia violence in Q4 2023 despite Operation Maliza Uhalifu.

Remote semi-arid county; Samburu pastoralism and wildlife tourism. ACLED's August 2023 report on Operation Maliza Uhalifu identifies Samburu as a primary militia hideout area. Peace agreement between Pokot and Samburu communities in November 2023.

Coastal-hinterland county; farming (Tana Delta) and pastoralism. OCHA's inter-communal conflict report (2014) identifies Tana River as one of Kenya's primary conflict hotspots; site of farmer-pastoralist massacres (2012-2013). ACLED records an active Kitui-Tana River border dispute as recently as 2023.

Kenya's largest and most arid county. ACLED's 2022 security assessment records Turkana with the highest fatalities of any Kenyan county that year driven by Pokot militia attacks on civilians. Extreme poverty, recurring drought, and cross-border conflict persist.

Northeastern tri-border county (Kenya-Ethiopia-Somalia). The primary economic activities include pastoralism and agriculture. Recurrent insecurity, especially along its borders with Somalia and Ethiopia, continues to undermine private investment, public service delivery, and broader economic diversification. ACLED identifies Mandera as the county with the highest number of al-Shabaab political violence events in both 2021 and 2022. Highest al-Shabaab attack frequency in Kenya.

Northeastern Puntland region; Bosaso port is the main commercial hub and economic center with a diversified economy that includes maritime trade, fisheries, livestock exports, mining, and commerce. OCHA records Bari as among the regions most affected by northern Somalia drought (2024-2025), with many boreholes out of service in Puntland and water prices nearly doubling. Relatively stable under Puntland administration with some Al-Shabaab rural presence.

Northwestern Somaliland region. Economy primarily based on livestock and agriculture. Severely drought-affected, with Global Acute Malnutrition doubling to 18% in hard-hit areas (OCHA, 2016). Stable under Somaliland governance since 1991. Displacement patterns are mostly intra-regional (99%) per IOM DTM data.

Puntland region; Garowe serves as Puntland's administrative capital.The main economic sustenance of the population is livestock. Nugaal is among regions with severe water shortages, with shallow wells dried up and health facilities non-operational during 2024-2025 drought. Relatively stable under Puntland governance; inter-clan tensions periodic.

Mountainous northeastern region; disputed between Somaliland and Puntland. The main economic sustenance of the population is livestock. OCHA's 2022 drought impact snapshot identifies Sanaag as experiencing deteriorating conditions with four out of eight most-affected districts. Nearly 43,000 people have been displaced by ongoing clan violence in Ceerigaabo town of Sanaag.

Somaliland's most populous region; capital Hargeisa is Somaliland's de facto capital. High flood vulnerability. Relatively stable and peaceful since 1991; trade, livestock, and services drive the economy.

Somaliland interior region; Burao is a major livestock trading hub. Periodic drought stress affecting pastoralists. IOM DTM displacement monitoring shows Togdheer displacement patterns are 96% intra-regional (mostly drought-driven), indicating relative stability.

Southwestern border region. The economy mostly depends on livestock and farming. IOM DTM Emergency Trend Tracking records Gedo as consistently among the most displacement-affected regions. Al-Shabaab maintains a significant and strategic presence across Gedo, controlling large rural swaths and several towns- including El Adde and Cows Qurun

Major banana and sorghum production region. Fertile agricultural zone; most conflict-affected Somali region per ACLED data (1,655 security incidents April 2023-July 2025, highest in the country). Al-Shabaab actively contests territory; military operations by Somali forces ongoing. Severe IDP displacement into Banadir.

Single-district region encompassing Mogadishu, Somalia's capital. Economy based largely on the export of agricultural products such as fruit, sugarcane, and corn, livestock, and animal products. ACLED records Banadir as second-most conflict-intensive region (955 security incidents April 2023-July 2025). High number of IDP receiving region.

Disputed region between Somaliland and Puntland. Pastoralism and cross-border trade. OCHA's 2022 drought snapshot lists Sool among most drought-affected regions with deteriorating conditions in two districts.

Central Shabelle River valley. The local economy faces volatility due to climatic shocks and relies heavily on remittances and livestock exports. Al-Shabaab active in rural areas while federal forces hold key towns.

Central Somalia; arid pastoralism and inter-clan conflict. ACLED records Galgaduud as the region with the highest cumulative fatalities in Somalia during April 2023-July 2025, driven by inter-clan violence and Al-Shabaab activity. Escalating interclan violence in Galgaduud is the primary driver of displacement.

Southwestern semi-arid region; subsistence farming along agropastoral livelihoods. Livestock serve as the main economic resource. OCHA situation reports document ongoing Al-Shabaab presence and food insecurity requiring humanitarian response.

Central region split between Puntland and Galmudug administrations. It features a rural economy based largely on pastoralist livelihoods and limited agriculture. The security environment is volatile due to inter-clan feuds and conflicting jurisdiction. Nearly 80,000 people newly displaced within weeks in February 2026 in Mudug due to drought.

Southern riverine region; agricultural potential along the Jubba River. The only region in Somalia fully controlled by Al-Shabaab. Al-Shabaab control of rural access routes; humanitarian access severely restricted. As of 2022, Middle Juba has the lowest Human Development Index score in the world out of 1,790 subnational regions.

Southernmost coastal region; port city of Kismayo serves as Jubaland's capital. Pastoralism. Key strategic importance to Al-Shabaab’s operations. The group conducted a number of attacks on military bases.

Southwestern agricultural heartland. Agro-pastoral economy. Bay was the third-most conflict-intensive Somali region between April 2023-July 2025 (756 security incidents). IOM DTM records Bay as major conflict and drought displacement source. Al-Shabaab active in rural areas.

Agricultural region; rice and sesame farming. Middle Shabelle was the second-highest in fatalities (2,480) in Somalia during April 2023-July 2025 period. Al-Shabaab has retaken towns previously cleared by Somali government forces. Military operations and inter-clan clashes drive ongoing displacement into Banadir.

Forested southwestern state; teak logging and subsistence farming. Over 165,000 people displaced since June 2025 due to conflict and airstrikes in Nagero, Tambura, Ezo, Mundri East, and Ibba counties. Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) incursions (2008-2017)  caused mass displacement; current government-community conflict ongoing.
Southeastern state; mixed farming and cattle herding. Intercommunal violence and  chronic insecurity are documented.

Northwestern state; cattle herding and subsistence farming. One of the most secured and politically stable states in South Sudan. High poverty rate with chronic food insecurity. Food security is heavily compromised by poor harvests due to irregular rainfall and flooding, high market prices, influx of returnees and new IDPs, as well as limited access to land in peri-urban IDP sites and relocation areas.

Contains Juba, the national capital. Subsistence farming and livestock production. In Central
Equatoria, some areas remain inaccessible due to insecurity caused by hold-out groups like the National Salvation Front (NAS). Cattle migration and land grabbing are major sources of conflict in Central Equatoria.

North-central state; cattle herding and subsistence farming. Wetland disputes, small arms proliferation, and poverty-driven cattle raiding have intensified violent conflict between Warrap and neighboring Unity and Lakes states.
Northwestern state; subsistence farming and cattle herding. High food insecurity and one of South Sudan's poorest states. The urgent restoration and scale?up of health services remains critical.

Oil-producing northeastern state; Paloch oilfields are critical to national revenue. High IDP rate. As of October 2025, the state accommodates over 225,000 IDPs. 73,000 people are facing starvation and death in Upper Nile and Jonglei states, where fighting between government forces and the Sudan People’s Liberation Army in Opposition has escalated since 2025. Risk of famine.
South-central state; pastoralism. High IDP rate. Lakes State classified as IPC Phase 4 (Emergency) in 2025 food security analysis. Chronic inter-clan violence documented since 2013.

Largest state by area: livestock, agriculture and fishing. Since the conflict began in late December 2025, approximately 1.35 million people in Jonglei State have lost access to essential health services due to damage, looting, and service disruptions. Cholera outbreak since September 2024. 73,000 people are facing starvation and death in Upper Nile and Jonglei states, where fighting between government forces and the Sudan People’s Liberation Army in Opposition has escalated since 2025.

Oil-rich northwestern state; agro-pastoralism. Epicenter of extreme atrocities during 2013-2018 civil war. The humanitarian and security situation in Unity State significantly deteriorated between January and February 2026. This period was marked by intense military confrontations between the South Sudan People’s Defence Forces (SSPDF) and the Sudan People’s Liberation Army-In Opposition (SPLA-IO).
